name: IvyEpic
classification_notes: IvyEpic is described as a fusion of geometric sans and grotesque traditions from the 1920s, with higher-than-usual contrast and tapered terminals that soften the geometry — placing it squarely at the intersection of geometric and grotesque sans-serif.
summary: A geometric grotesque drawing on 1920s precedents, distinguished by tapered tails and terminals that open up counters and lend warmth across 36 styles and a variable version.
description: Built on the formal overlap between geometric sans and early grotesque, IvyEpic carries more contrast than most contemporary sans-serifs. Its tapered tails and terminals keep counters open at heavier weights, producing a friendlier texture suited to both body text and display setting. The superfamily spans 36 styles across standard and condensed widths, with italics throughout and a variable font covering weight and width axes.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyEpic as a fusion of the geometric sans and grotesque from the 1920s, with higher contrast than a typical sans serif for better legibility at small sizes. The most distinctive features are the tapered tails and terminals, which keep counters open in heavier weights for a warm, friendly tone. With 36 total styles plus a variable version, IvyEpic is positioned as a versatile sans for editorial, corporate, and new media work.

name: IvyGothic
classification_notes: Derived from Times Gothic in the American Type Founders 1912 specimen book, IvyGothic sits squarely in the American grotesque tradition, rationalised for contemporary use with improved readability and simplified forms.
summary: A rationalised revival of Times Gothic from the American Type Founders 1912 specimen, refined by Jan Maack for clarity and modern use across nine weights.
description: Jan Maack took Times Gothic from the 1912 American Specimen Book of Type Styles and subjected it to a systematic clean-up, preserving its subtle warmth while ironing out historical irregularities. The result is a nine-weight grotesque spanning Thin to Black with matching italics, plus a variable font covering the full range via weight and slant axes. Positioned as a Helvetica alternative with more character, it suits editorial, branding and screen work where a neutral American grotesque with warmth is required.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyGothic as a rationalized redesign of Times Gothic from the American Specimen Book of Type Styles, a 1912 American Type Founders catalogue. Designer Jan Maack aimed to preserve the original's subtle warmth while refining its irregularities to improve readability, clarity and simplicity. Available in nine weights from Thin to Black with italics and as a variable font, the foundry position it as a great alternative to Helvetica.

name: IvyBodoni
classification_notes: IvyBodoni is a modern serif in the Bodoni/Didone tradition, characterised by extreme stroke contrast, crisp hairlines, ball terminals and precise curves drawn in homage to mid-century display designs such as Pistilli Roman and CBS Didot.
summary: A 21st-century Bodoni drawn by Jan Maack with crisp hairlines and ball terminals, informed by mid-century display faces such as Pistilli Roman and CBS Didot.
description: Designed with fashion and editorial typography in mind, IvyBodoni takes its cues from the high-contrast modern serifs of the 1960s and 1970s, along with lettering artists Mortimer Leach and Tommy Thompson. The family spans six weights from Thin to Bold across standard and condensed widths, with matching italics and two variable fonts covering both weight and width axes. OpenType features include old-style numerals, discretionary ligatures and multiple stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyBodoni as an homage to the modern serif designs of the 1960s and 1970s, citing Pistilli Roman, Didi and CBS Didot as references, alongside the lettering artists Mortimer Leach and Tommy Thompson. Designer Jan Maack drew crisp hairlines, distinct ball terminals and precise curves for a typeface made expressly for fashion and editorial display typography, available in six weights, two widths and as variable fonts.

name: IvyJournal
classification_notes: Draws on Roman inscriptional capitals and metal type traditions including Bembo, Baskerville and Times New Roman, placing it squarely in the transitional serif lineage adapted for contemporary text use.
summary: A contemporary workhorse serif by Jan Maack, drawing on Bembo, Baskerville and Times New Roman for use in books, magazines and office contexts.
description: Designed by Jan Maack, IvyJournal began as part of the IvyStyle type system before developing into a standalone family. The design applies principles of Roman inscriptional capitals and classic metal types to produce a versatile text serif across five weights with matching italics. Its range and opentype feature set, including old-style numerals, discretionary ligatures and fractions, make it a practical alternative to the more heavily used text faces it references.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyJournal as applying the principles of Roman inscriptional capitals and classic metal types such as Bembo, Baskerville and Times New Roman to a contemporary workhorse serif. Originally intended for the IvyStyle type system, Jan Maack's design evolved into a standalone family suited equally to books, magazines and office use.

name: IvyMazarin
classification_notes: A revival of a 1920s French interpretation of Garamond, placing it firmly in the old-style serif tradition with humanist roots and classical proportions refined for contemporary use.
summary: A 2026 revival of Robert Girard's 1921 Astrée, later issued as Mazarin by Stephenson Blake, drawing on rare archival specimens to reconstruct a French Garamond interpretation with cool crispness and a confident 12° italic slant.
description: Jan Maack worked from rare archival specimens to revive Astrée/Mazarin, refining proportions, raising the x-height and moderating ascenders while retaining the cool sharpness of the original italics. Historical quirks are preserved as OpenType features: a long-legged R, alternate Q, swashes, discretionary ligatures and small caps. Five weights with matching italics and variable fonts make it well suited to branding, editorial design and book typography.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyMazarin as a 2026 revival of Robert Girard's 1921 Astrée, later issued as Mazarin by Stephenson Blake in 1926, and a French 1920s interpretation of Garamond. Designer Jan Maack worked from rare archival specimens, preserving the original's cool crispness while refining proportions and setting a confident 12° italic slant. Historical quirks including a long-legged R, alternate Q and swashes remain available as features, with the family targeting branding, editorial and book typography.

name: IvyGrotesque
classification_notes: A grotesque sans rooted in the Stephenson Blake Grotesque No. 9 from 1906, with eccentric details tempered for even colour while retaining period warmth.
summary: A revival of Stephenson Blake Grotesque No. 9 from 1906, tightened for typographic layering across three widths and seven weights.
description: Rooted in Stephenson Blake Grotesque No. 9, the design reins in the original's more eccentric details to produce an even texture without losing its period warmth. The family spans three widths — regular, condensed and compressed — each in seven weights with matching italics, plus a variable font with slant, width and weight axes. Space-saving proportions make it well suited to visually dense layouts such as magazines, posters and book covers.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyGrotesque as a distilled homage to the Stephenson Blake Grotesque No. 9 from 1906, with eccentric details gently reined in to deliver an even tone while preserving warmth and character. The Thin Compressed master draws on Stephenson Blake Grotesque No. 7 for a coherent look across widths. Built on space-saving principles, the family suits visually layered typographic layouts such as magazines, posters and book covers, and is available in three widths and six weights with italics and a variable font supporting three axes.

name: IvyMode
classification_notes: IvyMode is a sans-serif with pronounced stroke contrast, subtly flared terminals that hint at serifs, and slightly angled stress — placing it firmly in the high-contrast or flared sans tradition rather than a conventional grotesque or humanist classification.
summary: A high-contrast flared sans by Jan Maack with diamond dots, bias-cut extenders and an unusually rich set of discretionary ligatures.
description: Designed by Jan Maack, IvyMode grew from a companion piece to IvyJournal into a fully independent family. Pronounced stroke contrast, subtly flared stroke endings, slightly angled stress and extenders cut on the bias give it a pressed, precise quality suited to fashion editorial and luxury branding. The OpenType feature set is notably generous, with stylistic sets, dozens of uppercase and lowercase discretionary ligatures, and both lining and old-style figures across ten styles plus two variable fonts.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyMode as beginning life as a high-contrast companion to IvyJournal before developing into its own distinct design. Jan Maack's sans hints at serifs through subtly flared stroke endings and features diamond dots, bias-cut extenders, angled stress, and an extended collection of novel ligatures and alternates that together give the face the crispness of a freshly ironed toile.

name: IvyPresto
classification_notes: IvyPresto is explicitly described as an Old Style (Garalde) family based on the work of Hendrik van den Keere, characterised by high contrast, sharp serifs, large x-height and narrow proportions.
summary: A Garalde serif drawn from Hendrik van den Keere's work, offered across three optical sizes covering display through to continuous text.
description: Based on the historical work of Hendrik van den Keere, IvyPresto combines a large x-height, sharp serifs and high contrast with narrow proportions suited to both display and text settings. The family spans 30 styles across Display, Headline and Text optical sizes, each available in five weights with matching italics. Cyrillic and Greek were added in May 2021 following consultation with Maria Doreuli, Krista Radoeva and Irene Vlachou.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyPresto as an Old Style (Garalde) family rooted in the works of Hendrik van den Keere. Its large x-height, sharp serifs, high contrast and narrow proportions give it strong readability across typographic settings. The family comprises three optical sizes — Display, Headline and Text — each with five weights in Roman and Italic, and was extended in May 2021 to include Cyrillic and Greek.

name: IvyOra
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a Dutch Old Face serif, revived from 17th-century historical specimens attributed to Nicolaes Briot and Christoffel van Dijck; sits between old style and transitional in character.
summary: A Dutch Old Face revival drawn from 17th-century specimens held at the Museum Plantin-Moretus, tracing letterforms attributed to Nicolaes Briot and Christoffel van Dijck.
description: Jan Maack of Ivy Foundry worked from a 1695 folio sheet, Proef van Letteren, printed by the widow of Dirck Voskens, to reconstruct the letterforms that also informed William Caslon's Great Primer Roman. The family splits into Display and Text optical sizes, each running five weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics, plus two variable fonts covering both weight and optical size axes. A thorough OpenType feature set includes small capitals, old-style figures, discretionary ligatures and fractions.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyOra as a Dutch Old Face serif revived from historical specimens at the Museum Plantin-Moretus in Antwerp. Designer Jan Maack treated it as a passion project, framing type revival as cultural preservation akin to restoring historic buildings or paintings. The primary source was a 1695 specimen sheet from the widow of Dirck Voskens, with roman letterforms attributed to Nicolaes Briot and the italic to Christoffel van Dijck, celebrated in his day as Holland's foremost punch-cutter of Old Face types.

name: IvyStyle Typewriter
classification_notes: Proportionally spaced rather than monospaced, with ball terminals and slab serifs drawing on typewriter aesthetics; firmly a contemporary slab serif rather than a true monospaced typewriter face.
summary: A proportionally spaced slab serif that borrows the aesthetic of typewriter type whilst giving each character room to breathe across ten styles.
description: Unlike conventional typewriter revivals, IvyStyle Typewriter uses proportional spacing so heavier weights stay readable without becoming dense. Distinctive ball terminals give it a warm, editorial character suited to long-form text. The family pairs with IvyStyle Sans for slab and sans combinations within the same superfamily.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyStyle Typewriter as a contemporary slab serif that takes cues from the typewriter genre without being monospaced, allowing each character to occupy the space it needs. Ball terminals give it a friendly editorial quality, and with ten styles it offers more versatility than typical typewriter faces. It is designed to pair with IvyStyle Sans for harmonious slab and sans combinations.

name: IvyStyle Sans
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly references the humanist genre, and the design combines constructed, modern geometry with the open apertures and warm detailing characteristic of humanist sans-serifs.
summary: A narrow humanist sans with a constructed, modern character and sufficient quirk to keep it from feeling anonymous.
description: Clean lines and open apertures give it strong reading comfort across editorial and branding contexts, while its narrow proportions make it a practical space-saver without sacrificing personality. The family runs across five weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic, and variable fonts are included with the full package. It is designed to pair with IvyStyle Typewriter for cohesive typographic systems.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvyStyle Sans as a constructed, modern design with the warm and welcoming voice of the humanist genre, noting enough quirky detail to give it an unobtrusive personality. They highlight its clean lines, open apertures and narrow proportions as making it a multifunctional sans suited to editorial design, branding and identity work, recommending it be paired with IvyStyle Typewriter.

name: IvySoft
classification_notes: IvySoft is a rounded sans-serif with tapered tails and rounded terminals, sharing skeletal DNA with IvyEpic but softened for a warmer, more relaxed register.
summary: A rounded sans-serif sharing structural roots with IvyEpic, offering a warmer, more relaxed alternative across 16 styles and a variable font.
description: Built from the same skeletal foundations as IvyEpic, IvySoft adds rounded terminals, tapered tails and a touch of stroke contrast to produce a sans-serif that reads cleanly at text sizes while carrying an approachable warmth. The family spans eight weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and is also available as a variable font covering both weight and slant axes. The shared lineage with IvyEpic makes the two families a natural pairing for projects that need tonal range across a single typographic system.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe IvySoft as an unpretentious, rounded sans that balances work and play. The skeletal structure is no-nonsense for tight, readable text, while rounded terminals, tapered tails and a degree of contrast give the overall look warmth. The design began with IvyEpic material, making the two families a natural pairing. Available in 16 styles with italics or as variable fonts.

name: SwingKing
classification_notes: SwingKing is a casual sans-serif drawn from hand-lettered sources and digitised for typographic use; its illustrative origins and accompanying icon set place it firmly in the display category rather than a workhorse sans.
summary: A casual sans drawn from the hand lettering of Danish illustrator Erik Sørensen, digitised and refined by Jan Maack, with an extensive companion icon set.
description: Jan Maack collaborated with Erik Sørensen, a Danish illustrator who spent decades unable to find a typeface that matched his comic strip aesthetic, to produce this digitised and polished casual sans. The result sits between cartoonish and handwritten without committing to either, making it usable for a range of informal applications. A dedicated Icons font adds dozens of symbols, pictograms and emojis that extend the family's expressive range.
foundry_blurb: Ivy Foundry describe SwingKing as a joyfully illustrated typeface born from a collaboration between Jan Maack and Danish illustrator Erik Sørensen, whose pre-digital career left him without a suitable typographic voice for his illustrations and comics. Maack digitised, polished and improved Sørensen's hand-drawn letters into a useful casual sans that is neither cartoonish nor handwritten, complemented by dozens of symbols, icons and emojis.
